extra.message: User input from request.GET.get('p') at line 39 is joined into a file path at line 42 via os.path.join(base_path, filename) and passed directly to open() without any sanitization or path validation. An attacker can use '../' sequences to traverse the filesystem and read arbitrary files (e.g., ?p=../../settings.py or ?p=../../../etc/passwd).

User input from request.POST.get('first_name') at line 68 is base64-decoded and passed directly to exec() at line 72 (Python 3 path) and line 76 (Python 2 path). This allows an attacker to execute arbitrary Python code on the server by submitting a base64-encoded payload as the 'first_name' form field.

Python 2 fallback path for the same code injection vulnerability. User input from request.POST.get('first_name') is base64-decoded and passed directly to exec() without any validation or sandboxing. This is the Python 2 execution path at line 76.

User input from request.GET.get('url') at line 177 is passed directly to Django's redirect() function at line 178 without any validation. An attacker can craft a URL like ?url=https://evil.com to redirect users to a malicious site, enabling phishing attacks.

User input from request.GET.get('fwd') at line 182 is used to look up and invoke arbitrary functions from the module's global namespace via globals().get(forward) at line 183 and function(request) at line 186. An attacker can call any function defined in this module (e.g., the hidden 'admin' function, or 'boom') by supplying its name as the 'fwd' parameter.

The template variable {{qs|safe}} at line 28 renders user input from request.GET.get('qs') (set in views.py:98) without HTML escaping due to the |safe filter. This value is placed inside an HTML input element's value attribute, allowing an attacker to inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript via the 'qs' query parameter (e.g., ?qs="><script>alert(1)</script>).

The template variable {{path|safe}} at line 49 renders the URL path parameter without HTML escaping due to the |safe filter. The 'path' variable comes from the URL pattern (?P<path>.+) and is passed to the template in views.py:105-106. An attacker can inject JavaScript via the URL path, which will be rendered inside a <script> block as var myvar = "<payload>";.

The template variable {{qs|safe}} at line 37 renders user input from request.GET.get('qs') (set in views.py:110) without HTML escaping due to the |safe filter. This value is placed inside a <script> block as var myvar = "<payload>";, allowing an attacker to break out of the string and inject arbitrary JavaScript via the 'qs' query parameter.

The template variable {{qs|safe}} at line 48 renders user input from request.GET.get('qs') (set in views.py:166) without HTML escaping due to the |safe filter. This value is placed inside a <script> block as var x = "<payload>";, allowing an attacker to inject arbitrary JavaScript. This is combined with the @csrf_exempt decorator on the view, making it exploitable for CSRF+XSS attacks.

The missing_access_control view at line 155-159 grants admin access based solely on the query parameter ?action=admin without any authentication or authorization check. Any unauthenticated user can access the admin interface by simply appending ?action=admin to the URL.

The dor_user_profile view at lines 128-139 allows any user to view and modify any other user's profile by changing the userid parameter in the URL (e.g., /direct-object-references/users/2). There is no authentication or authorization check to verify the requesting user owns the profile being accessed or modified.

cwe: CWE-639: Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key

extra.message: The CSRF middleware 'django.middleware.csrf.CsrfViewMiddleware' is commented out at line 102 in MIDDLEWARE_CLASSES. This disables CSRF protection globally for the entire application, making all POST form submissions vulnerable to cross-site request forgery attacks.

The csrf_image view at line 165 is explicitly decorated with @csrf_exempt at line 164, disabling any CSRF protection even if the middleware were enabled. Combined with the XSS vulnerability in the template ({{qs|safe}}), this enables cross-site request forgery attacks.

DEBUG is set to True at line 10. When deployed to production, this exposes detailed stack traces, SQL queries, and internal application state to any user who triggers an error. The boom() view at views.py:143-144 intentionally raises exceptions that exploit this to expose sensitive information.

cwe: CWE-209: Information Exposure Through an Error Message

extra.message: The Django SECRET_KEY is hardcoded in source code at line 90 as 'h++jszpm)i@p%ay_b=cp#()^od!qns14)h%@qm3)p=cuo+st^a'. This key is used for cryptographic signing of sessions, CSRF tokens, and other security-critical operations. A leaked secret key allows attackers to forge sessions and tamper with signed data.

The XFrameOptionsMiddleware is commented out at line 106, disabling clickjacking protection. Without X-Frame-Options headers, the application's pages can be embedded in iframes on malicious sites, enabling clickjacking attacks.

cwe: CWE-1021: Improper Restriction of Rendered UI Layers or Frames

extra.message: The Django Debug Toolbar is enabled and its visibility is controlled by a user-controllable show_toolbar() function at lines 180-185 that checks request.GET.get('debug'), request.POST.get('debug'), or request.COOKIES.get('debug'). Any user can enable the debug toolbar by adding ?debug=1 to any URL, exposing SQL queries, settings, HTTP headers, and other sensitive internal application state.

cwe: CWE-200: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor

extra.message: The boom() view at lines 143-144 intentionally raises an unhandled exception. Combined with DEBUG=True in settings.py, this exposes a detailed Django error page containing the full stack trace, local variables, settings, and installed middleware to any user who visits /misconfiguration/boom.

cwe: CWE-209: Information Exposure Through an Error Message
